source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.1.4 DNS (pp. 234-235)

options: Translates (resolves) human-friendly domain names into IP addresses
questions.why: DNS (Domain Name System) maps hostnames/domain names to IP addresses; the translation is called domain name resolution.

questions.q: For docs.rockylinux.org, what is 'docs' and what is 'rockylinux.org'?
options: 'docs' is the hostname; 'rockylinux.org' is the domain
'docs' is the domain; 'rockylinux.org' is the hostname
questions.why: The hostname identifies the device (docs) while rockylinux.org is the domain; a domain does not represent a specific host.

options: A Fully Qualified Domain Name - the complete name of a host plus all domain levels (e.g. docs.rockylinux.org.)
questions.why: The FQDN is the full hostname-plus-domains name; per RFC the trailing root dot is standard (docs.rockylinux.org.).

questions.q: In docs.rockylinux.org., what is the root domain?
questions.why: The root domain is the final dot; org is the first-level (TLD), rockylinux the second-level, docs the hostname.

questions.q: How many canonical root-server endpoints are there, and how are they realised physically?
options: 13 logical endpoints (a-m.root-servers.net), implemented by 1,500+ anycast instances worldwide
Exactly 13 physical servers, one per continent
questions.why: There are 13 logical root endpoints (a through m) but they are served by over 1,500 anycast instances run by 13 organisations.

options: A contiguous portion of the DNS namespace managed by an authoritative server, holding its resolution records (A, MX, ...)
questions.why: A zone is the slice of the namespace an authoritative server manages, storing the FQDN records within that scope.
